texts.label: text
texts.orig: Medu and Mishti read 'thought of the day' on the school noticeboard every day. Today's thought, 'annena jatani jivanti', makes them curious. Mishti tells Medu that it is a Sanskrit saying which means 'food gives life to living beings.'
texts.text: Medu and Mishti read thought of the day on the school noticeboard every day. Today's thought, 'annena_ jatani jivanti', makes them _ curious. Mishti tells Medu that it is a Sanskrit saying which means 'food gives life to living beings.'

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us try to understand the significance of this saying.
texts.text: Let us try to understand the significance of this saying.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1 What Do We Eat?
texts.text: 3.1 What Do We Eat?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All of us eat food every day. Food is an essential component of our daily life. List the food items you have consumed over the week in Table 3.1.
texts.text: All of us eat food every day. Food is an essential component of our daily life. List the food items you have consumed over the week in Table 3.1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What observations can you make about your food from the data collected in Table 3.1? Do you eat the same kind of food in every meal or do your choices vary? Compare your list with those prepared by your friends. Find similarities and differences in the food consumed by you and your friends. What did you find? Record your findings in your notebook. You may have noticed that there is a variety in the food eaten by you and your friends.
texts.text: What observations can you make about your food from the data collected in Table 3.1? Do you eat the same kind of food in every meal or do your choices vary? Compare your list with those prepared by your friends. Find similarities and differences in the food consumed by you and your friends. What did you find? Record your findings in your notebook. You may have noticed that there is a variety in the food eaten by you and your friends.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Do you think that such diversity in food exists in all states of our country?
texts.text: Do you think that such diversity in food exists in all states of our country?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1.1 Food in different regions
texts.text: 3.1.1 Food in different regions

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 3.2: Let us explore
texts.text: Activity 3.2: Let us explore

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Find out the types of food traditionally consumed and the crops grown in various states of India. You may refer to books in your library, search the internet, and interact with your friends, family and neighbours to collect information.
texts.text: ¢ Find out the types of food traditionally consumed and the crops grown in various states of India. You may refer to books in your library, search the internet, and interact with your friends, family and neighbours to collect information.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢@ InTable 3.2, add more states and fill the collected data. A few examples are already given.
texts.text: ¢@ InTable 3.2, add more states and fill the collected data. A few examples are already given.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Why do we see diversity in traditional food consumed in various states of our country?
texts.text: Why do we see diversity in traditional food consumed in various states of our country?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Analyse the data collected by you in Table 3.2. Are there food items that are common across states? Make a list of those food items. You may find that some food items are common in many states while some are eaten only in a particular state.
texts.text: Analyse the data collected by you in Table 3.2. Are there food items that are common across states? Make a list of those food items. You may find that some food items are common in many states while some are eaten only in a particular state.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What relation do you find between the traditional food items and the locally grown crops? You must have observed that the traditional food of any state is usually based on the crops grown in that state. India is an agricultural country with diverse soil and climate types. Various crops are grown in its different regions depending on the soil types and climatic conditions.
texts.text: What relation do you find between the traditional food items and the locally grown crops? You must have observed that the traditional food of any state is usually based on the crops grown in that state. India is an agricultural country with diverse soil and climate types. Various crops are grown in its different regions depending on the soil types and climatic conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In various regions of India, the choice of food may vary according to the cultivation of food crops in that particular region, taste preferences, culture, and traditions.
texts.text: In various regions of India, the choice of food may vary according to the cultivation of food crops in that particular region, taste preferences, culture, and traditions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3 | 2 How have cooking practices changed over time?
texts.text: 3 | 2 How have cooking practices changed over time?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You have learnt that food habits vary across states. Our food choices as well as practices of food preparation may differ from one another. Have our food habits and cooking practices changed over time?
texts.text: You have learnt that food habits vary across states. Our food choices as well as practices of food preparation may differ from one another. Have our food habits and cooking practices changed over time?

texts.orig: Activity 3.3: Let usinteract and find oUC
texts.text: Activity 3.3: Let usinteract and find oUC

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Prepare a list of questions for gathering information from elderly people about their food habits and cooking practices. Following are some of the sample questions—
texts.text: ¢ Prepare a list of questions for gathering information from elderly people about their food habits and cooking practices. Following are some of the sample questions—

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: = What kind of food do you still eat and what is new?
texts.text: = What kind of food do you still eat and what is new?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: = Whatare the changes in cooking practices over time?
texts.text: = Whatare the changes in cooking practices over time?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: = What has caused these changes?
texts.text: = What has caused these changes?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Conduct interviews with some elderly people based on the questions prepared.
texts.text: ¢ Conduct interviews with some elderly people based on the questions prepared.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: (a) Chulha (Traditional stove)
texts.text: (a) Chulha (Traditional stove)

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: (b) Modern gas stove
texts.text: (b) Modern gas stove

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: (c) Sil-batta (Stone grinder)
texts.text: (c) Sil-batta (Stone grinder)

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: (d) Electrical grinder
texts.text: (d) Electrical grinder

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 3.1: Change in cooking tools over time
texts.text: Fig. 3.1: Change in cooking tools over time

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What are your findings from the interviews you conducted? Cooking practices, also called culinary practices, have changed over time. There is a significant difference between traditional and modern culinary practices. Earlier, most cooking was done using a chulha (Fig. 3.1a). Nowadays, most of us cook using a modern gas stove (Fig. 3.1b). Earlier, most grinding was done manually using a sil-batta (Fig. 3.1c). These days, we use an electrical grinder for ease of grinding (Fig. 3.1d). Find out what were the other ways of cooking and grinding. Why have these culinary practices changed over time? These changes may be due to factors such as technological development, improved transportation and better communication.
texts.text: What are your findings from the interviews you conducted? Cooking practices, also called culinary practices, have changed over time. There is a significant difference between traditional and modern culinary practices. Earlier, most cooking was done using a chulha (Fig. 3.1a). Nowadays, most of us cook using a modern gas stove (Fig. 3.1b). Earlier, most grinding was done manually using a sil-batta (Fig. 3.1c). These days, we use an electrical grinder for ease of grinding (Fig. 3.1d). Find out what were the other ways of cooking and grinding. Why have these culinary practices changed over time? These changes may be due to factors such as technological development, improved transportation and better communication.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2 What are the Components of Food?
texts.text: 3.2 What are the Components of Food?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Medu and Mishti visit the 'Traditional Food Festival' organised in their school. The theme of the festival is 'Eat Healthy, Live Healthy'.
texts.text: Medu and Mishti visit the 'Traditional Food Festival' organised in their school. The theme of the festival is 'Eat Healthy, Live Healthy'.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The festival features various stalls displaying different kinds of traditional dishes. Dr Poshita, a nutritional expert, explains to students that 'Health is the Ultimate Wealth'.
texts.text: The festival features various stalls displaying different kinds of traditional dishes. Dr Poshita, a nutritional expert, explains to students that 'Health is the Ultimate Wealth'.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mindful Eating: A Path to a Healthy Body
texts.text: Mindful Eating: A Path to a Healthy Body

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us understand what Dr Poshita means by this statement.
texts.text: Let us understand what Dr Poshita means by this statement.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Have you ever missed a meal? How do you feel when you miss a meal?
texts.text: Have you ever missed a meal? How do you feel when you miss a meal?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We feel tired and less energetic when we do not eat for some time. Why do you think a marathon runner drinks glucose water during and after a race?
texts.text: We feel tired and less energetic when we do not eat for some time. Why do you think a marathon runner drinks glucose water during and after a race?

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 3.2: Some sources of carbohydrates
texts.text: Fig. 3.2: Some sources of carbohydrates

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Glucose provides __instant energy. Glucose is an example of a carbohydrate. Carbohydrates are one of the primary sources of energy in our diet. Cereals like wheat, rice, and maize, vegetables like potato and sweet potato, and fruits like banana, pineapple, and mango are some sources of carbohydrates (Fig. 3.2).
texts.text: Glucose provides __instant energy. Glucose is an example of a carbohydrate. Carbohydrates are one of the primary sources of energy in our diet. Cereals like wheat, rice, and maize, vegetables like potato and sweet potato, and fruits like banana, pineapple, and mango are some sources of carbohydrates (Fig. 3.2).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Do you know that common sugar is also a type of carbohydrate?
texts.text: Do you know that common sugar is also a type of carbohydrate?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Why do you think we prefer to have laddoos as a part of our traditional diet in winters?
texts.text: Why do you think we prefer to have laddoos as a part of our traditional diet in winters?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Besan or wheat flour (aata) and ghee are among the main ingredients of laddoos along with goond (edible gum), nuts, and seeds. Ghee and various kinds of oils are grouped ~~ under another kind of food component, which is called fat.
texts.text: Besan or wheat flour (aata) and ghee are among the main ingredients of laddoos along with goond (edible gum), nuts, and seeds. Ghee and various kinds of oils are grouped under another kind of food component, which is called fat.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Yes, my grandma told me that laddoos rich in ghee and nuts provide energy to keep us warm.
texts.text: Yes, my grandma told me that laddoos rich in ghee and nuts provide energy to keep us warm.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sources of fats can be from plants or animals (Fig. 3.3). Nuts, such as groundnuts, walnuts, coconuts, and almonds, and seeds, such as pumpkin seeds and sunflower seeds, are some sources of fat. Fat is a source of stored energy.
texts.text: Sources of fats can be from plants or animals (Fig. 3.3). Nuts, such as groundnuts, walnuts, coconuts, and almonds, and seeds, such as pumpkin seeds and sunflower seeds, are some sources of fat. Fat is a source of stored energy.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 3.3: Some sources of fats
texts.text: Fig. 3.3: Some sources of fats

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbohydrates and fats provide us energy for performing various activities. Therefore, they are called energy-giving foods. Identify more food items that are rich sources of carbohydrates and fats .
texts.text: Carbohydrates and fats provide us energy for performing various activities. Therefore, they are called energy-giving foods. Identify more food items that are rich sources of carbohydrates and fats .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proteins are also an important part of our food. Milk products and pulses are good sources of protein. Sportspersons need proteins in larger quantities to build their muscles. People get proteins from plants as well as animals. Some excellent plant sources of protein are pulses, beans, peas and nuts (Fig. 3.4a). Animal sources of protein are milk, paneer, egg, fish and meat (Fig. 3.4b). Protein-rich foods help in growth and repair of our body. These are, therefore, called body-building foods.
texts.text: Proteins are also an important part of our food. Milk products and pulses are good sources of protein. Sportspersons need proteins in larger quantities to build their muscles. People get proteins from plants as well as animals. Some excellent plant sources of protein are pulses, beans, peas and nuts (Fig. 3.4a). Animal sources of protein are milk, paneer, egg, fish and meat (Fig. 3.4b). Protein-rich foods help in growth and repair of our body. These are, therefore, called body-building foods.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 3.4: Some sources of proteins
texts.text: Fig. 3.4: Some sources of proteins

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The right amount of protein must be included in the diet of growing children for their proper growth and development. Which of these food components are part of your daily diet?
texts.text: The right amount of protein must be included in the diet of growing children for their proper growth and development. Which of these food components are part of your daily diet?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Have you ever seen mushrooms? They grow : : . mostly in dark and moist places. Edible mushrooms are good sources of protein.
texts.text: Have you ever seen mushrooms? They grow mostly in dark and moist places. Edible mushrooms are good sources of protein.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Mushroom
texts.text: Mushroom

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Why do you think we are advised to include servings of fruits, vegetables and other plant-based foods in our daily diet? Let us understand the importance of some other food components by reading the following two cases—
texts.text: Why do you think we are advised to include servings of fruits, vegetables and other plant-based foods in our daily diet? Let us understand the importance of some other food components by reading the following two cases—

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Case 1
texts.text: Case 1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In earlier times, during long voyages, sailors often suffered from bleeding and swollen gums. During a voyage in 1746, Scottish physician James Lind observed that sailors who consumed lemons and oranges recovered from these symptoms. Bleeding and swollen gums are symptoms of a disease called scurvy.
texts.text: In earlier times, during long voyages, sailors often suffered from bleeding and swollen gums. During a voyage in 1746, Scottish physician James Lind observed that sailors who consumed lemons and oranges recovered from these symptoms. Bleeding and swollen gums are symptoms of a disease called scurvy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What do you interpret by reading Case 1? What cures scurvy? Lemons and oranges help in curing scurvy. Scurvy is caused due to deficiency of Vitamin C. Vitamin C present in citrus fruits like lemons and oranges helps in curing this disease.
texts.text: What do you interpret by reading Case 1? What cures scurvy? Lemons and oranges help in curing scurvy. Scurvy is caused due to deficiency of Vitamin C. Vitamin C present in citrus fruits like lemons and oranges helps in curing this disease.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Case 2
texts.text: Case 2

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: What do you infer from Case 2?
texts.text: What do you infer from Case 2?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You may have learnt about iodised salt through newspapers, advertisements or by reading about it on a salt packet. What does it mean? Iodised salt is simply common salt mixed with required quantities of salts of iodine.
texts.text: You may have learnt about iodised salt through newspapers, advertisements or by reading about it on a salt packet. What does it mean? Iodised salt is simply common salt mixed with required quantities of salts of iodine.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: How would you find out more about other food components that protect our body from various diseases?
texts.text: How would you find out more about other food components that protect our body from various diseases?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Study the chart given in Fig. 3.5 to explore the functions and sources of various food components. Find out more sources of vitamins and minerals. Also, understand the symptoms of the diseases caused by the lack of these food components.
texts.text: ¢ Study the chart given in Fig. 3.5 to explore the functions and sources of various food components. Find out more sources of vitamins and minerals. Also, understand the symptoms of the diseases caused by the lack of these food components.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Visit your neighbourhood, interact with people and find out if any individual shows the symptoms listed on the chart (an investigatory project of this kind can be taken by the students under the guidance of a teacher).
texts.text: ¢ Visit your neighbourhood, interact with people and find out if any individual shows the symptoms listed on the chart (an investigatory project of this kind can be taken by the students under the guidance of a teacher).

texts.orig: ¢ Correlate these symptoms with their diet and identify the deficiency disease(s) or disorder(s).
texts.text: ¢ Correlate these symptoms with their diet and identify the deficiency disease(s) or disorder(s).

texts.orig: @ Suggestthe possible cause(s) for the symptoms observed and changes required in the diet for improvement.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From Fig. 3.5, you have learnt that vitamins (A, B,, C and D) and minerals (calcium, iodine, and iron) are two groups of food components that protect our body from various diseases. But, how can we overcome vitamin and mineral deficiency diseases or disorders?
texts.text: From Fig. 3.5, you have learnt that vitamins (A, B,, C and D) and minerals (calcium, iodine, and iron) are two groups of food components that protect our body from various diseases. But, how can we overcome vitamin and mineral deficiency diseases or disorders?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Vitamin
texts.text: Vitamin

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: (b) Some sources of Vitamin B,
texts.text: (b) Some sources of Vitamin B,

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 3.6: Some sources of different vitamins
texts.text: Fig. 3.6: Some sources of different vitamins

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Food components that provide energy, support growth, help repair and protect our body from diseases, and maintain various bodily functions are called nutrients. The major nutrients in our food include carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins and minerals.
texts.text: Food components that provide energy, support growth, help repair and protect our body from diseases, and maintain various bodily functions are called nutrients. The major nutrients in our food include carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins and minerals.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: milk, green vegetables, fruits and wholegrains regularly. These food items are some sources of vitamins (Fig.3.6) and minerals. Although vitamins and minerals are required in small amounts, they are essential to keep our body healthy.
texts.text: milk, green vegetables, fruits and wholegrains regularly. These food items are some sources of vitamins (Fig.3.6) and minerals. Although vitamins and minerals are required in small amounts, they are essential to keep our body healthy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What differences do you observe in raw and cooked vegetables? Have you ever noticed that vegetables sometimes lose their bright colour, or become softer and less crisp when cooked? Some nutrients like vitamin C and others are lost during cooking due to high heat. Would it not be wise to include fruits and uncooked vegetables into our diet? Washing cut or peeled vegetables and fruits may also result in the loss of some vitamins. However, it is highly recommended that all fruits and vegetables be thoroughly washed before consumption.
texts.text: What differences do you observe in raw and cooked vegetables? Have you ever noticed that vegetables sometimes lose their bright colour, or become softer and less crisp when cooked? Some nutrients like vitamin C and others are lost during cooking due to high heat. Would it not be wise to include fruits and uncooked vegetables into our diet? Washing cut or peeled vegetables and fruits may also result in the loss of some vitamins. However, it is highly recommended that all fruits and vegetables be thoroughly washed before consumption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fruits and vegetables are rich in dietary fibres. Let us see how dietary fibres are beneficial for us.
texts.text: Fruits and vegetables are rich in dietary fibres. Let us see how dietary fibres are beneficial for us.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to the essential nutrients, our body needs dietary fibres and water. Dietary fibres, also known as roughage, do not provide any nutrients to our body. However, they are an essential component of our food. They help our body get rid of undigested food and ensure smooth passage of stools. Roughage in our food is provided mainly by suitable plant products.
texts.text: In addition to the essential nutrients, our body needs dietary fibres and water. Dietary fibres, also known as roughage, do not provide any nutrients to our body. However, they are an essential component of our food. They help our body get rid of undigested food and ensure smooth passage of stools. Roughage in our food is provided mainly by suitable plant products.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Green leafy vegetables, fresh fruits, wholegrains, pulses and nuts are good sources of roughage.
texts.text: Green leafy vegetables, fresh fruits, wholegrains, pulses and nuts are good sources of roughage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Eating food that is locally grown and plant based, to the extent possible, is not only healthy for the body but is also good for our environment and our planet.
texts.text: Eating food that is locally grown and plant based, to the extent possible, is not only healthy for the body but is also good for our environment and our planet.

texts.label: text

texts.text: has difficulty in oe I understand avised her to in fibre. My grandm passing SO tor W hy the doctor au a food that 1s high

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Water is also an essential part of our diet. It helps the body absorb nutrients from food. It removes waste from the body through sweat and urine. We should drink sufficient water regularly to keep ourselves healthy.
texts.text: Water is also an essential part of our diet. It helps the body absorb nutrients from food. It removes waste from the body through sweat and urine. We should drink sufficient water regularly to keep ourselves healthy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Coluthur Gopalan (1918-2019) initiated nutrition research in India. He analysed more than 500 Indian foods for their nutritional value and recommended an appropriate diet in the Indian context. He led surveys on the nutritional status of the Indian population, identifying
texts.text: Coluthur Gopalan (1918-2019) initiated nutrition research in India. He analysed more than 500 Indian foods for their nutritional value and recommended an appropriate diet in the Indian context. He led surveys on the nutritional status of the Indian population, identifying

texts.label: text
texts.orig: widespread deficiencies in protein, energy, and other food components. This led to the implementation of the Mid Day Meal Programme in 2002, now a 'PM POSHAN' initiative, to provide balanced food in the government-run and government-aided schools of our country. This scheme has played a role in improving the health and nutrition of millions of children nationwide.
texts.text: widespread deficiencies in protein, energy, and other food components. This led to the implementation of the Mid Day Meal Programme in 2002, now a 'PM POSHAN' initiative, to provide balanced food in the government-run and government-aided schools of our country. This scheme has played a role in improving the health and nutrition of millions of children nationwide.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3 How to Test Different Components of Food?
texts.text: 3.3 How to Test Different Components of Food?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us find out which nutrients are present in various food items.
texts.text: Let us find out which nutrients are present in various food items.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some nutrients like starch (a type of carbohydrate), fat and protein can be detected using fairly simple tests, while others can be detected only in a well-equipped laboratory. Let us explore how we can detect the presence of starch, fat and protein in some food items.
texts.text: Some nutrients like starch (a type of carbohydrate), fat and protein can be detected using fairly simple tests, while others can be detected only in a well-equipped laboratory. Let us explore how we can detect the presence of starch, fat and protein in some food items.

texts.orig: > 3 1 Test for starch

texts.orig: ¢ Takeasmall quantity of the food items such as a slice of potato, cucumber, bread, some boiled rice, boiled gram, crushed peanuts, oil, butter and crushed coconut. You can take other food items too for testing.

texts.text: Fig. 3.7: Testing for the presence of starch in various food items

texts.orig: ¢ Place a small piece of each item on a separate dish.
texts.text: ¢ Place a small piece of each item on a separate dish.

texts.orig: @ Observe if there are any changes in the colour of the food items. Have they turned blue-black? Record your observations in Table 3.3.
texts.text: @ Observe if there are any changes in the colour of the food items. Have they turned blue-black? Record your observations in Table 3.3.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Take a small part of the food items that you tested for the presence of starch in Activity 3.5.
texts.text: ¢ Take a small part of the food items that you tested for the presence of starch in Activity 3.5.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Place each food item on a separate piece of paper.
texts.text: @ Place each food item on a separate piece of paper.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Wrap the paper around the food and press it. Be careful not to tear the paper.
texts.text: @ Wrap the paper around the food and press it. Be careful not to tear the paper.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Ifa food item contains a little water, allow the paper to dry.
texts.text: ¢ Ifa food item contains a little water, allow the paper to dry.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Does the paper develop an oily patch? What do you think is the reason for this patch? If oil or butter is present in the food item, it leaves an oily patch on the paper. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some foods have high calories due to high sugar and fat content. Moreover, they contain very low amounts of proteins, minerals, vitamins, and dietary fibres. These foods are called junk foods. These foods include potato wafers, candy bars and carbonated drinks. Consuming these foods frequently is not good as these are not healthy for our body. They make a person obese. Such a person may suffer from several health problems. You should always remember Dr Poshita's statement that 'Health is the Ultimate Wealth.' We should take care of our body to stay healthy. Eating a balanced diet and avoiding junk food contribute towards

These foods are called junk foods. These foods include potato wafers, candy bars and carbonated drinks. Consuming these foods frequently is not good as these are not healthy for our body. They make a person obese. Such a person may suffer from several health problems. You should always remember Dr Poshita's statement that 'Health is the Ultimate Wealth.' We should take care of our body to stay healthy. Eating a balanced diet and avoiding junk food contribute towards

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Packaged food items must have information about the nutrients on their cover. The information should list the amount of each nutrient. Sometimes, more nutrients are added to the food during processing (fortification) to improve its nutritional quality. Iodised salt and some baby foods are examples of fortified foods. The Food Safety and Standard Authority of India (FSSAI) is a government agency that regulates food quality in India.
texts.text: Packaged food items must have information about the nutrients on their cover. The information should list the amount of each nutrient. Sometimes, more nutrients are added to the food during processing (fortification) to improve its nutritional quality. Iodised salt and some baby foods are examples of fortified foods. The Food Safety and Standard Authority of India (FSSAD is a government agency that regulates food quality in India.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.5 Millets: Nutrition-rich Cereals
texts.text: 3.5 Millets: Nutrition-rich Cereals

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You may have heard of jowar, bajra, ragi, and sanwa (Fig. 3.9). These are native crops of India (Fig. 3.9). These can be easily cultivated in different climatic conditions. These highly nutritious grains are also called millets. Have you ever had food items made from these millets?
texts.text: You may have heard of jowar, bajra, ragi, and sanwa (Fig. 3.9). These are native crops of India (Fig. 3.9). These can be easily cultivated in different climatic conditions. These highly nutritious grains are also called millets. Have you ever had food items made from these millets?

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 3.9: Sanwa (Barnyard millet)
texts.text: Fig. 3.9: Sanwa (Barnyard millet)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Millets are small-sized grains and have been an integral part of the Indian diet for centuries. They have regained popularity due to their numerous health benefits. They are good sources of vitamins, minerals like iron and calcium, and dietary fibres as well. That is the reason they are also called nutri-cereals. They contribute significantly to a balanced diet required for the normal functioning of our body.
texts.text: Millets are small-sized grains and have been an integral part of the Indian diet for centuries. They have regained popularity due to their numerous health benefits. They are good sources of vitamins, minerals like iron and calcium, and dietary fibres as well. That is the reason they are also called nutri-cereals. They contribute significantly to a balanced diet required for the normal functioning of our body.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: How does food reach from a farm to our plate? What are the steps involved in this process? Who are the people involved in this process? Do you know how much time and effort is required to get the wheat flour once seed grains germinate in the farm? Let us look at Fig. 3.10 to understand the entire process of making the chapati that we eat.
texts.text: How does food reach from a farm to our plate? What are the steps involved in this process? Who are the people involved in this process? Do you know how much time and effort is required to get the wheat flour once seed grains germinate in the farm? Let us look at Fig. 3.10 to understand the entire process of making the chapati that we eat.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 3.10: From farm to plate
texts.text: Fig. 3.10: From farm to plate

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The entire distance travelled by a bag of wheat or any other food item, from the producer to the consumer, is known as its food miles. Reducing food miles is important because it helps to cut down the cost and pollution during its transport; it helps support local farmers; and it also keeps our food fresh and healthy.
texts.text: The entire distance travelled by a bag of wheat or any other food item, from the producer to the consumer, is known as its food miles. Reducing food miles is important because it helps to cut down the cost and pollution during its transport; it helps support local farmers; and it also keeps our food fresh and healthy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Many people waste food, leaving it unconsumed on their plates. One must remember the time and effort put by our farmers and other community members in getting the food from the farm to our plate. We must take only as much food as we can consume. It would reduce food wastage. Try to find the timeline for the various processes

texts.orig: Eat healthy, share, and respect food. Support local producers!
texts.text: Eat healthy, share, and respect food. Support local producers!

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ People across India eat diverse types of food, containing various food components.
texts.text: @ People across India eat diverse types of food, containing various food components.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Choice of food may vary according to the cultivation of food crops in a region, taste preferences, culture and traditions, and so on.
texts.text: ¢ Choice of food may vary according to the cultivation of food crops in a region, taste preferences, culture and traditions, and so on.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Culinary practices have changed over time. There is a significant difference between traditional and modern methods of cooking food.
texts.text: Culinary practices have changed over time. There is a significant difference between traditional and modern methods of cooking food.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Food provides us energy, support growth, repairs our bodies and protects us from diseases.
texts.text: Food provides us energy, support growth, repairs our bodies and protects us from diseases.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: The major nutrients in our food are carbohydrates, fats, proteins, vitamins, and minerals. In addition, food also contains dietary fibres and water.
texts.text: The major nutrients in our food are carbohydrates, fats, proteins, vitamins, and minerals. In addition, food also contains dietary fibres and water.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Carbohydrates and fats are primary energy sources, while proteins are body-building nutrients.
texts.text: Carbohydrates and fats are primary energy sources, while proteins are body-building nutrients.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Vitamins and minerals strengthen our body, protect us from infections, and keep us healthy.
texts.text: Vitamins and minerals strengthen our body, protect us from infections, and keep us healthy.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: A balanced diet provides all the essential nutrients in the right quantities, along with adequate roughage and water.
texts.text: A balanced diet provides all the essential nutrients in the right quantities, along with adequate roughage and water.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Deficiency of one or more nutrients in our diet for a long time can lead to deficiency diseases and disorders.
texts.text: Deficiency of one or more nutrients in our diet for a long time can lead to deficiency diseases and disorders.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Junk foods are unhealthy as they contain high levels of sugar and fats but little protein, minerals, vitamins, and dietary fibres.
texts.text: Junk foods are unhealthy as they contain high levels of sugar and fats but little protein, minerals, vitamins, and dietary fibres.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Millets are known as nutri-cereals as they provide most of the nutrients required for the normal functioning of our bodies. They can be easily cultivated in different climatic conditions.
texts.text: Millets are known as nutri-cereals as they provide most of the nutrients required for the normal functioning of our bodies. They can be easily cultivated in different climatic conditions.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Eating food that is locally grown and plant-based, to the extent possible, is not only healthy for our bodies but is also good for our environment and our planet.
texts.text: Eating food that is locally grown and plant-based, to the extent possible, is not only healthy for our bodies but is also good for our environment and our planet.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: The distance travelled by a food item, from the place of its production to the consumer, is called food miles. We must aim to minimise food miles.
texts.text: The distance travelled by a food item, from the place of its production to the consumer, is called food miles. We must aim to minimise food miles.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: We should never waste food and only take as much as we can consume.
texts.text: We should never waste food and only take as much as we can consume.

Collect more information from the internet about their salt making process and the need to make their own salt. Collect pictures and paste them on a chart paper. Also, write a paragraph about the process of making this salt and its usefulness.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Vegetables or fruits that grow naturally in the forest or nearby fields without being cultivated by farmers are considered wild varieties. Traditionally, many tribal groups in India depend on these wild varieties, which form a part of their food. Read about ranbhajis from Maharashtra and edible mushrooms from Himachal Pradesh. Are you aware of any such wild varieties of food from your region? Discuss in class.
texts.text: Vegetables or fruits that grow naturally in the forest or nearby fields without being cultivated by farmers are considered wild varieties. Traditionally, many tribal groups in India depend on these wild varieties, which form a part of their food. Read about ranbhajis from Maharashtra and edible mushrooms from Himachal Pradesh. Are you aware of any such wild varieties of food from your region? Discuss in class.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Notes
texts.text: Notes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the 1960s, Indian scientists found that among the human population in the Himalayan region and the Northern plains of India, symptoms of swelling at the front of the neck were prevalent. As per norms of the Government of India, an effort was made to supplement common salt with iodine for preparing iodised salt. Consumption of iodised salt visibly reduced the above symptoms. These symptoms were due to a deficiency of iodine in the soil of this region resulting in a lack of iodine in the local food and water supply. Swelling at the front of the neck is a symptom of a disease called goitre.
texts.text: In the 1960s, Indian scientists found that among the human population in the Himalayan region and the Northern plains of India, symptoms of swelling at the front of the neck were prevalent. As per norms of the Government of India, an effort was made to supplement common salt with iodine for preparing iodised salt. Consumption of iodised salt visibly reduced the above symptoms. These symptoms were due to a deficiency of iodine in the soil of this region resulting in a lack of iodine in the local food and water supply. Swelling at the front of the neck is a symptom of a disease called goitre.

texts.text: Polar bear

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polar bears accumulate a lot of fat under their skin. This fat serves as an energy source. It supports them during their monthslong winter sleep (hibernation), enabling them to survive without eating.
texts.text: Polar bears accumulate a lot of fat under their skin. This fat serves as an energy source. It supports them during their monthslong winter sleep (hibernation), enabling them to survive without eating.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Salt farming is a traditional practice of a tribal community named Agariyas. They practice salt farming in the Little Rann of Kutch and other parts of Gujarat. For eight months, they live in the extreme heat of the desert and work very hard to get salt from seawater.
texts.text: Salt farming is a traditional practice of a tribal community named Agariyas. They practice salt farming in the Little Rann of Kutch and other parts of Gujarat. For eight months, they live in the extreme heat of the desert and work very hard to get salt from seawater.
